name: SQLAlchemy
what_it_does: The Python SQL toolkit and ORM. SQLAlchemy provides two layers: Core (SQL expression language for building queries programmatically) and ORM (Python class-to-table mapping with relationships, lazy loading, sessions). SQLAlchemy 2.0 modernized the API with Python 3.7+ typing and async support. Paired with Alembic for migrations. The standard database toolkit for Python, used by FastAPI, Flask, and countless Python applications.
use_cases: Map Python classes to database tables with SQLAlchemy ORM and manage relationships (one-to-many, many-to-many)
Build complex SQL queries programmatically with SQLAlchemy Core's expression language
Use with FastAPI and async SQLAlchemy (async_engine, AsyncSession) for async database access
Manage schema migrations with Alembic (SQLAlchemy's migration companion) for version-controlled schema changes
Support multiple databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite, Oracle) with the same application code via dialect abstraction
not_for: MongoDB or NoSQL databases — SQLAlchemy is relational databases only
Highest performance bulk operations — SQLAlchemy's ORM layer adds overhead; use raw SQL with psycopg2 for extreme bulk throughput
Simple scripts that just need one or two queries — raw SQL via sqlite3 or psycopg2 is simpler
best_when: You're building Python applications that need a full-featured ORM with relationship management, migrations, and cross-database compatibility.
avoid_when: You need MongoDB/NoSQL, or you want the simplest possible raw SQL access — psycopg2 or sqlite3 are simpler.

auth.notes: Library with no auth — database credentials in connection URL.

pricing.notes: Free and open source, maintained by Michael Bayer (zzzeek) and community.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — local library. ORM adds overhead; use Core for performance-critical paths.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: SQLAlchemy 2.0 provides typed errors for common issues. Session state errors have descriptive messages. Relationship loading errors include the relationship name and loading strategy.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Read queries are idempotent. merge() provides idempotent upsert for ORM objects.
known_agent_gotchas: SQLAlchemy 2.0 changed the API from 1.x — session.execute() now returns Row objects, not tuples; session.query() is legacy; new code must use select() statement API
ORM sessions are not thread-safe — use scoped_session() or one session per thread/request; never share Session between concurrent requests
Lazy loading relationships outside the session context raises DetachedInstanceError — load relationships eagerly (joinedload, selectinload) or access within session scope
N+1 query problem: accessing ORM relationships in loops triggers a query per object — use selectinload() or joinedload() in query options to eager load
Async SQLAlchemy requires AsyncEngine and AsyncSession — regular Engine/Session blocks the event loop in async context; don't mix sync and async SQLAlchemy
SQLAlchemy Column types are not Python types — Integer, String, DateTime are SQLAlchemy types not Python int/str/datetime; use Mapped[int] for type annotations in 2.0 style

security_score_components.security_notes: Parameterized queries prevent SQL injection in both Core and ORM. text() literals can inject SQL — use bindparams() with text(). Connection credentials in env variables. TLS via database connection URL parameters.
